perturbation.original_text: 4. Choice of Law. All matters relating to arbitration will be governed by the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. Sections 1 et seq.). Except to the extent governed by the Federal Arbitration Act or other federal law, this Agreement and all claims arising from the relationship between Hyatt (and/or any of its Affiliates) and Playa (and/or any of its Affiliates) under this Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Illinois (U.S.A.), without regard to its conflict of laws rules, except that any Illinois law or any other law regulating the offer or sale of franchises, business opportunities, or similar interests, or governing the relationship between a franchisor and a franchisee or any similar relationship, will not apply unless its jurisdictional requirements are met independently without reference to this Section 4.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Choice of Law. All matters relating to arbitration will be governed by the Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. Sections 1 et seq.). Except to the extent governed by the Federal Arbitration Act or other federal law, this Agreement and all claims arising from the relationship between Hyatt (and/or any of its Affiliates) and Playa (and/or any of its Affiliates) under this Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Illinois (U.S.A.), without regard to its conflict of laws rules.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception clause, the contract could be interpreted as applying Illinois franchise laws to the agreement even when those laws' jurisdictional requirements are not independently met. This is particularly problematic because the agreement involves international parties and properties outside of Illinois. Franchise laws often have specific registration and disclosure requirements that would not normally apply to this international agreement but now could be argued to.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the explicit exclusion of Illinois franchise law, potentially subjecting the agreement to the Illinois Franchise Disclosure Act, even if jurisdictional requirements are not met independently. This act mandates specific disclosures and registration requirements for franchises operating within Illinois or with Illinois residents, which could create substantial compliance issues and potential liabilities for Hyatt or Playa if the agreement is interpreted as falling under the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly excludes the application of Illinois franchise laws if their jurisdictional requirements are not independently met. The changed text removes this exception, potentially subjecting the agreement to those laws even if the jurisdictional requirements are not met. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that franchise laws often have registration and disclosure requirements. The Law Explanation states that the Illinois Franchise Disclosure Act could create compliance issues and potential liabilities for Hyatt or Playa. Since the removal of the exception opens the door to potential legal issues, the change creates a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not relevant, as it discusses the Illinois - Indiana Bridge Commissioners Act.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Waiver of Non-compensatory Damages. In any action or proceeding between the Parties (including any arbitration proceeding) arising under or with respect to this Agreement or in any manner pertaining to the Hyatt All-Inclusive Resorts or to the relationship of the Parties under this Agreement, each Party hereby unconditionally and irrevocably waives and releases any right, power or privilege either may have to claim or receive from the other Party any punitive or exemplary damages, each Party acknowledging and agreeing that the remedies herein provided and other remedies at law or in equity will in all circumstances be adequate. Both Parties acknowledge that they are experienced in negotiating agreements of this sort, and have had the advice of counsel in connection with, and fully understand the nature of, the waiver contained in this Section 14.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Waiver of Non-compensatory Damages. In any action or proceeding between the Parties (including any arbitration proceeding) arising under or with respect to this Agreement or in any manner pertaining to the Hyatt All-Inclusive Resorts or to the relationship of the Parties under this Agreement, each Party hereby unconditionally and irrevocably waives and releases any right, power or privilege either may have to claim or receive from the other Party any punitive or exemplary damages, each Party acknowledging and agreeing that the remedies herein provided and other remedies at law or in equity will in all circumstances be adequate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes an acknowledgement that both parties are experienced and have had advice of counsel regarding the waiver. Removing this weakens the enforceability of the waiver, potentially violating consumer protection laws if a party later argues they didn't understand the implications. This is especially relevant if one party could be considered to have significantly less bargaining power.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the acknowledgement of counsel and experience in negotiating such agreements, the waiver of punitive damages could be challenged under the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act, 815 ILCS 505/2, if one party argues they were misled or did not fully understand the implications of the waiver. The Act prohibits unfair or deceptive acts in trade or commerce, and a poorly explained or understood waiver, particularly if there's a power imbalance, could be deemed a violation.

Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices, including but not limited to the use or employment of any deception fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation or the concealment, suppression or omission of any material fact, with intent that others rely upon the concealment, suppression or omission of such material fact, or the use or employment of any practice described in Section 2 of the "Uniform Deceptive Trade Practices Act", approved August 5, 1965, in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful whether any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby. In construing this section consideration shall be given to the interpretations of the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts relating to Section 5 (a) of the Federal Trade Commission Act. (Source: P.A. 78-904 .) perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the acknowledgement that both parties are experienced in negotiating agreements of this sort and have had the advice of counsel. This omission is significant because it weakens the enforceability of the waiver, potentially violating consumer protection laws. The Law Explanation suggests that without this acknowledgement, a party could argue they were misled or did not fully understand the implications of the waiver. This argument is further substantiated by the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act, 815 ILCS 505/2, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts in trade or commerce, including the concealment, suppression, or omission of any material fact. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that "the concealment, suppression or omission of any material fact, with intent that others rely upon the concealment, suppression or omission of such material fact...in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful whether any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby." Removing the acknowledgement of counsel weakens the protection against potential claims of being misled, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Dispute Resolution. (a) All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall to the extent possible be settled amicably by negotiation between the Parties within fifteen (15) days from the date of written notice by either Party of the existence of such dispute, and, failing such amicable settlement, shall be finally settled by arbitration under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce ("Rules"). To the extent there is any conflict between the Rules and the Federal Arbitration Act as it pertains to such arbitration, the Rules shall prevail.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Dispute Resolution. (a) All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall to the extent possible be settled amicably by negotiation between the Parties within fifteen (15) days from the date of written notice by either Party of the existence of such dispute, and, failing such amicable settlement, shall be finally settled by arbitration under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce ("Rules").
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'To the extent there is any conflict between the Rules and the Federal Arbitration Act as it pertains to such arbitration, the Rules shall prevail,' the agreement now creates potential conflict. FAA generally preempts conflicting state law. By omitting FAA law, and given that this agreement is written to be governed by the laws of the State of Illinois, opens up the dispute resolution procedure to conflicting Illinois state law regarding arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the clause prioritizing the ICC Rules over conflicting aspects of the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) creates a contradiction. The FAA generally preempts state laws that conflict with its provisions regarding arbitration agreements. By omitting the explicit statement that the ICC Rules prevail over conflicting aspects of the FAA, the agreement opens itself up to potential challenges under conflicting Illinois state law regarding arbitration, potentially undermining the enforceability of the arbitration agreement as intended under federal law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause prioritizing ICC Rules over conflicting aspects of the FAA creates a contradiction. The FAA, as highlighted in both scraped law snippets (9 USC 2), establishes the validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate. By removing the explicit statement about the ICC Rules prevailing, the agreement potentially undermines the FAA's intended effect and opens itself to state law challenges, weakening the enforceability of the arbitration agreement under federal law. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition because it removes essential information, creating legal loopholes concerning the prioritization of arbitration rules.
